screens.title: You Used a Lever Today (and Didn't Notice)
screens.content:
When you opened a door, you used a lever. When you opened a can of soda with a pull-tab, you used a lever. When you pried open a stuck lid with a spoon, you used a lever.

A lever is humanity's oldest machine — invented thousands of years ago — and your robot needs to understand it to be strong.

Archimedes said: "Give me a lever long enough and a fulcrum on which to place it, and I shall move the world."

screens.title: Lever Basics
screens.content:
A lever has three parts:

1. **The Load** — The thing you're trying to move (a door, a lid, a rock)
2. **The Fulcrum** — The pivot point (a hinge, the edge of a can, a pebble under the crowbar)
3. **The Effort** — The force you apply (pushing on the door handle, pulling the pull-tab, pushing down on the crowbar)

The magic of a lever is **mechanical advantage**. You can move something heavy by applying a small force far from the fulcrum.

The formula is simple:
**Mechanical Advantage = Distance from fulcrum to effort / Distance from fulcrum to load**

If your effort is 2 meters from the fulcrum and the load is 0.5 meters away, you get 4x mechanical advantage. A 10 kg load feels like 2.5 kg.

screens.title: Three Classes of Levers
screens.content:
Levers come in three flavors, and they're all around you:

**CLASS 1: Fulcrum in the middle**
Load — Fulcrum — Effort
Example: A seesaw, a crowbar, scissors, pliers
Advantage: Can gain mechanical advantage by moving the fulcrum

**CLASS 2: Load in the middle**
Fulcrum — Load — Effort
Example: A wheelbarrow, a bottle opener, a nutcracker, a door on its hinges
Advantage: Always gives you mechanical advantage

**CLASS 3: Effort in the middle**
Load — Effort — Fulcrum
Example: Tweezers, a fishing rod, your arm when you lift something
Advantage: You lose mechanical advantage but gain speed and range

Robots use all three types depending on their job. A robot arm might use Class 1 joints for precision, Class 2 for lifting heavy objects, and Class 3 to move quickly.

screens.title: Experiment With Lever Balance
screens.content: Adjust the fulcrum position in this interactive lever. Watch how mechanical advantage changes!

screens.title: Designing a Robot Gripper
screens.content:
Your robot needs to pick up delicate objects (like eggs) without crushing them, but also grip heavy objects firmly.

The gripper uses a Class 2 lever (the mechanical advantage is built in). The problem: if the effort fingers move 1 cm, the load fingers only move 0.1 cm. You need gentle, precise movement.

Solution? Use a motor with a gearbox that moves slowly (we'll learn about gears next lesson). Combine the gearbox's slow speed with the lever's mechanical advantage, and your robot can grip anything from a grape to a boulder.

screens.title: Build the Best Crowbar
screens.content:
You need to pry open a 200 kg steel safe door. You can apply 100 kg of force. The safe door edge (the load) is 0.2 meters from the pivot point. How far from the pivot must you place the fulcrum so that your force is enough to open it?

Use the lever simulator to find the answer.

params.hintShow: You need a mechanical advantage of at least 2. Adjust the effort distance.

question.prompt: You're designing a robot arm to move slowly but with very high force. Where should you place the fulcrum relative to the load and effort?

options.text: Close to the load, far from the effort
options.feedback: Exactly! When the fulcrum is close to the load and far from the effort, you get high mechanical advantage. The load barely moves, but with enormous force. Perfect for heavy lifting.

options.text: Close to the effort, far from the load
options.feedback: That would give you a low mechanical advantage. The load would move a lot but with little force. Better for speed than strength.
That gives you a 1:1 ratio — no advantage at all. You'd need to apply as much force as the load weighs.

options.text: It doesn't matter; all fulcrum positions are the same
options.feedback: Fulcrum position is everything! It determines your mechanical advantage.
